coredata.dc:title: Efficient construction of self-assembled starch colloidosomes for controlled-release of pesticides
coredata.prism:publicationName: Colloids and Surfaces A: Physicochemical and Engineering Aspects

coredata.dc:creator: Huang, Xudong
Liu, Wenbiao
Zheng, Zhidan
Zhang, Yimeng
Zhao, Xia
Li, Zequan
Gao, Wei
Xie, Peng
Pan, Yuanfeng
Sun, Qian
coredata.dc:description: Biodegradable colloidosomes have attracted increasing attention for their applications to enhance the efficacy and reduce wastage of pesticides in agriculture. In this study, we present a continuous and highly efficient method for production of starch colloidosomes. The starch particles with an average particle size of 230.2 nm and good dispersibility were prepared using surface modification technology, and then used as environment-friendly shell materials to form self-assembled starch colloidosomes using spray drying technology. The influences of inlet temperature, feed rate and air flow rate on the morphologies of colloidosomes were also studied. This strategy enables the construction of starch colloidosomes with uniform spherical morphology and controllable pore sizes under different pH, and can be used for pesticide encapsulation. After loading pyraclostrobin (PYR), the regular spherical colloidosomes not only achieve a small mean diameter of 2.35 µm, but also realize a high encapsulation efficiency of 82 %. The release performance of PYR has an obvious pH response and follows the segmented Ritger-Pepapes model of sustained-release. The colloidosomes were used to prevent the photodegradation of pesticides. The PYR loaded colloidosomes exhibit excellent UV resistance with a retention rate of 72.7 %. In addition, the loading of Fe3O4 nanoparticles and the embedding of carbon dots were also explored.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Starch colloidosomes
Controlled-release
Pesticides
Spray drying technology

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of starch colloidosomes using spray drying technology
Preparation of Fe3O4 or CDs loaded colloidosomes
Expanded applications for loading of Fe3O4 or CDs

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Preparation process of the starch dispersion.
Preparation process of the starch colloidosomes using a spray drying method.
Schematic diagram of starch particle formation.

(a) Average size of starch particles measured by DLS and (b) their Zeta potential at different SDS concentrations, (c) TEM image and size distribution of starch particles under 0.6 g/L SDS condition and (d) FT-IR spectra of soluble starch, SDS, gelatin and starch particles.
(a) Schematic diagram of spray drying and (b) the colloidosomes drying process, (c) effect of internal and external pressure on colloidosomes morphology during the drying process.

SEM images of colloidosomes at different conditions: (a1–3) inlet temperature: 140 °C, 160 °C and 180 °C, (b1–3) feed rate: 4 mL/min, 6 mL/min and 8 mL/min, (c1–3) gas flow rate: 5 m3/min, 10 m3/min and 12 m3/min, and size distribution of colloidosomes prepared under different conditions: (d1–3) inlet temperature, feed rate and gas flow rate.

(a) SEM image and (b) size distribution of PYR loaded colloidosomes, (c) FT-IR spectra of PYR, blank colloidosomes and PYR loaded colloidosomes, (d) PYR release performance of pure PYR and PYR colloidosomes with different loading capacity, (e) PYR release performance of colloidosomes under different pH conditions, and (f) their fitting curves.
(a) pH response mechanism of colloidosomes, and (b) CLSM images of colloidosomes infiltrated by FITC-dextran.
(a) Schematic representation of the photostability properties of PYR and PYR loaded colloidosomes, (b) graph of the cumulative remaining rate of PYR over time after UV irradiation.

(a) TEM image and particle size distribution of the modified Fe3O4, (b) SEM image of the Fe3O4 loaded colloidosomes, (c) FT-IR spectra of the modified Fe3O4 and Fe3O4 colloidosomes with different loading rates, (d) EDS elemental spectroscopy images of Fe3O4 loaded colloidosomes, (e) hysteresis return curves of the modified Fe3O4 and Fe3O4 loaded colloidosomes with different loading rates and the digital photograph of adsorption process, and (f) schematic diagram of adsorption of Fe3O4 loaded colloidosomes using a magnet.

(a) TEM image and particle size distribution of CDs, (b) HAADF-STEM image of CDs, (c) SEM image of CD loaded colloidosomes and photograph of CDs solution and CD loaded colloidosomes solution under 365 nm irradiation, (d) localized magnification of CD loaded colloidosomes, (e) FT-IR spectra of CDs, blank colloidosomes, and CD loaded colloidosomes, (f) XRD spectra of blank and CD loaded colloidosomes.

head.ce:title: Efficient construction of self-assembled starch colloidosomes for controlled-release of pesticides

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Biodegradable colloidosomes have attracted increasing attention for their applications to enhance the efficacy and reduce wastage of pesticides in agriculture. In this study, we present a continuous and highly efficient method for production of starch colloidosomes. The starch particles with an average particle size of 230.2 nm and good dispersibility were prepared using surface modification technology, and then used as environment-friendly shell materials to form self-assembled starch colloidosomes using spray drying technology. The influences of inlet temperature, feed rate and air flow rate on the morphologies of colloidosomes were also studied. This strategy enables the construction of starch colloidosomes with uniform spherical morphology and controllable pore sizes under different pH, and can be used for pesticide encapsulation. After loading pyraclostrobin (PYR), the regular spherical colloidosomes not only achieve a small mean diameter of 2.35 µm, but also realize a high encapsulation efficiency of 82 %. The release performance of PYR has an obvious pH response and follows the segmented Ritger-Pepapes model of sustained-release. The colloidosomes were used to prevent the photodegradation of pesticides. The PYR loaded colloidosomes exhibit excellent UV resistance with a retention rate of 72.7 %. In addition, the loading of Fe3O4 nanoparticles and the embedding of carbon dots were also explored.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Pesticides are widely used in agriculture to control pests and plant diseases, to increase the yield and quality of crop production[1]. When using conventional emulsifiable concentrate formulation of pesticides, there exists some problems, such as low usage efficiency and environmental pollution[2,3], due to pesticide photodegradation and solvent volatilization[4,5]. Developing an eco-friendly and efficient pesticide formulation is crucial for minimizing chemical residues, thereby ensuring crop and ecological safety[6]. Controlled release formulations (CRFs) are pesticide delivery systems that encapsulate or adsorb the pesticide active ingredient through polymers or inorganic nanoparticles to mitigate the process of pesticide decomposition and loss in non-target environments[7–9]. For instance, matrine-loaded silica nanoparticles reduced the number of nymph N. lugens by 55 % and increased the biomass of rice shoots by 74.8 %[10]. The development of CRFs holds significant importance in enhancing pesticide efficacy, minimizing residual pollution and ensuring the sustainability of agricultural systems [11].

Microcapsules are micrometer-sized containers, with polymer or inorganic shells, that can be used to protect the core materials from harsh environments and are widely used in agriculture applications, especially as a type of CRFs[12]. The traditional preparation methods of microcapsules include in-situ polymerization[13], interfacial polymerization[14], solvent evaporation[15] and phase separation polymer precipitation method[16]. The microcapsules prepared by these methods may have high entrapment efficiency and uniform morphology. However, these methods involve using large amounts of organic solvents and complicated preparation processes. To solve the above issues, the concept of colloidosomes was proposed. Colloidosomes are Pickering emulsion microcapsules whose shells consist of fused or coagulated self-assembled colloidal particles with uniform porosities[17]. In 1996, Velev et al[18]. firstly introduced colloidosomes, using polystyrene particles as shell materials by an emulsion template method. Subsequently, Noble et al. self-assembled colloidosomes from epoxy microbars using a template method[19]. Later, Duan et al.[20], Lawrence et al.[21], Routh et al.[22], and other researchers [23–25] extended the preparation and application of colloidosomes. However, most colloidosome shells are polymer materials, such as methyl methacrylate [26], polyamide[27], and polystyrene [28]. These non-degradable shells will cause secondary pollution in the agricultural environment when used as pesticide carriers. More importantly, conventional colloidosomes can only release pesticides slowly rather than controllably on demand, and their self-assembly processes are difficult for large scale preparation, which may limit their practical applications. Therefore, it is important to develop an efficient and green approach for preparing environmentally friendly colloidosomes with excellent environment responsive and controllable release properties.

Stimulus-responsive colloidosomes can respond to small changes in the environment, such as temperature[15,29], pH[6,30], and light[31], which can achieve customized delivery and have targeting properties to improve the utilization rate of pesticides. For instance, in the alkaline gut of a pest, the rate of drug release needs to be accelerated to kill the pest as quickly as possible. Conversely, in an acidic dew environment, the rate of drug release needs to be reduced in order to minimize pesticide loss [32]. Therefore, it is necessary to construct colloidosomes as pesticide carriers with pH stimulation response.

Spray drying is a rapid and scalable drying technology, which can be applied for drying liquid raw materials into solid powders by atomization in a stream of hot dry air[33,34]. It is widely used in food [35], pharmacy [36], cosmetics[37] and many other fields[38]. In a typical drying process, the liquid raw materials, such as solutions, dispersions, or even pastes, are atomized by a nozzle[39]. In a drying chamber, water or solvents can be rapidly evaporated to obtain dry products with higher chemical and physical stability[40]. This technology can also be used to prepare self-assembled polymer shell colloidosomes with a particle size of 0.5–50 μm [41].

In this study, highly dispersed starch particles were prepared as environmentally friendly shell materials using surface modification, and the self-assembled colloidosomes were constructed by a one-step method using spray drying. Pyraclostrobin (PYR) is used as a model pesticide, which is a broad-spectrum and highly effective pesticide. The PYR loaded colloidosomes were used for controlled release and UV resistance tests. In addition, the functional nanoparticle loaded colloidosomes, including Fe3O4 and carbon dots (CDs), were similarly constructed using this spray drying strategy.

Soluble starches were purchased from Shanghai Bide Pharmaceutical Technology Co., Ltd. Gelatin, ferric chloride hexahydrate (FeCl3·6 H2O), sodium citrate dihydrate (Na3Cit·2 H2O), sodium dodecyl sulfate (SDS), and glycerol were obtained from Guangdong Guanghua Technology Co., Ltd. Pyraclostrobin (PYR, 97 %) was bought from Shanghai Yuanye Biotechnology Co., Ltd. Ferrous chloride tetrahydrate (FeCl2·4 H2O) from Shanghai Macklin Co., Ltd. Deionized water was filtered by a water puriﬁcation system (Smart-30) from Shanghai Hitech Instruments Co., Ltd. Fluorescein isothiocyanate-labeled dextran (FITC-dextran) with a molecular weight of 100 kDa was ordered from Xi’an Ruixi Biological Technology Co., Ltd. Ammonia hydroxide (NH3·H2O) was supplied from Chongqing Chuandong Chemical Co., Ltd and hydrochloric acid (HCl), methanol (CH3OH, ≥99.5 %) and ethanol (C2H5OH, ≥99.7 %) were supplied from Chengdu Cologne Chemical Co., Ltd. The Fe3O4 nanodispersions and CDs were made in the laboratory[42,43]. The Spray Dryer (L-317) was produced by Beijing Laiheng Technology Co., Ltd. The ultrasonic homogenizer (SCIENTZ-IID) was bought from Ningbo Xinzhi Biotechnology Co., Ltd.

The starch particles were prepared using in situ modification combined with ultrasound, as shown in Fig. 1. Briefly, the soluble starch solution (1 g, 80 mL) was dextrinized at 95 °C, 400 rpm for 30 min, and then SDS solution (0.06 g, 10 mL) was added dropwise to the pasted starch solution with stirring for another 30 min. Gelatin solution (0.05 g, 10 mL) and 1 mL of propanetriol were added sequentially to the solution, and the temperature was lowered to 50 °C after 30 min, using an ultrasonic cell pulverizer to sonicate the mixed solution for 10 min to obtain highly dispersed starch particles.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of starch colloidosomes using spray drying technology
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 2 shows the preparation of starch colloidosomes by spray drying. Before spray drying, the starch particles were mixed with methanol at a volume ratio of (1:3). Firstly, the mixed solution was conveyed under certain conditions of feed temperature, gas flow rate and feed rate, and then atomized into tiny droplets through a nozzle. The water or methanol in the droplets was evaporated in a drying chamber. The dried products were separated by a cyclone and fell into a collection chamber. The products were collected and stored at room temperature.

PYR methanol solution (0.33 g/L) was prepared and then mixed with starch solution and water at a volume ratio of 4:1:1. The mixed solution was atomized in a nozzle at an inlet temperature of 160 °C, a flow rate of 10 L/h and a feed rate of 6 mL/min. After temperature stabilization, the sample solution was fed through a peristaltic pump to obtain PYR loaded colloidosomes in the collection chamber. These colloidosomes were then stored in vials protected from light.

The encapsulation efficiency of PYR in colloidosomes was measured and calculated by dissolving 100 mg PYR loaded colloidosomes in 50 mL of methanol. The methanol solution of colloidosomes was put into a cell crusher and sonicated for 2 min (300 W), and 5 mL of the methanol solution was filtered through a 0.22 μm filter membrane. The absorbance was measured at 276 nm with a UV spectrophotometer, in which the highest absorbance was taken as Amax. A T was the theoretical absorbance value and the encapsulation efficiency (EE) was calculated according to Eq. 1.

500 mg of the sample was packed in a non-woven filter bag and immersed in 100 mL of aqueous methanol solution of different pH or temperature (methanol: water = 2:1). At regular intervals, the buffer solution (5 mL) was taken and its absorbance was measured at 276 nm. The corresponding fresh aqueous methanol mixture (5 mL) of the same pH or temperature value was then added. The cumulative release content of PYR was calculated according to Eq. 2. The release data were fitted using zero-order, first-order, Higuchi, and Ritger-Peppas models.
(1)
E E =
A
m a x
A T
× 100 %
(2)
Cumulative release =
V e
∑ 1
n − 1
C i
+
V 0
C n
m PYR
× 100 %

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of Fe3O4 or CDs loaded colloidosomes
ce:section.ce:para: The Fe3O4 nanoparticles were prepared according to our previous work[42]. In order to obtain Fe3O4 loaded colloidosomes, the Fe3O4 (1 wt%) and starch dispersions (1 wt%) were mixed to form solutions with a Fe3O4 solid content of 5 %, 10 % and 15 % at an inlet rate of 4 mL/min, and the rest of the parameters were the same as in Section 2.4.

The CD nanoparticles were prepared on the basis of the previous work[43]. To obtain CD colloidosomes, the CD solutions (1 wt%) were prepared and then mixed with starch dispersions (1 wt%) according to the volume ratio of 1:4, and then the mixture was atomized to obtain CD colloidosomes. The spray drying parameters were the same as those of Fe3O4 colloidosomes.

The morphology and dispersibility of starch particles were evaluated by transmission electron microscopy (TEM, HT-7700, Hitachi) at an accelerating voltage of 80 kV, and the morphology and dispersibility of Fe3O4 and CDs were evaluated by transmission electron microscopy (TEM, JEOL JEM 2100 F, JEOL) at an accelerating voltage of 200 kV. The morphology and microstructure of colloidosomes were examined using a scanning electron microscope (SEM, FEI Quattro S, Thermo Electron) at an accelerating voltage of 10 kV or 15 kV. The particle size measurements of SEM and TEM images were performed using Nano Measurer software. The particle size and Zeta potential of starch particles were measured by dynamic light scattering (DLS, Zetasizer Nano ZS90, Malvern). The absorbances of released PYR were tested with a UV–vis spectrophotometer (UV–vis, UV670, Meipuda) at a resolution of 0.5 nm. The fluorescence properties of colloidosomes were imaged by confocal laser scanning microscopy (CLSM, FV1200, Olympus). Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR, Nicolet iS10, Thermo Fisher Scientific) was used to characterize the chemical composition of starch granules and colloidosomes using the KBr disc technique in the range of 4000–400 cm−1. The hysteresis return curves of Fe3O4 and Fe3O4 loaded colloidosomes were measured using a vibrating sample magnetometer (VSM, 7410, Lake Shore Cryotronics). The crystallographic structure was investigated by X-ray diffraction (XRD, A24A10, Breker) with Cu Kα radiation (λ=1.54 Å) and 40 KV/30 mA. The retentions of PYR during the photodegradation process were estimated by an ultraviolet analyzer (ZF-23max, Xiulab) at the wavelength 254 nm with a power of 16 W.

Fig. 3 displays a schematic diagram of starch particle formation. Initially, the starch suspensions are pasted, and then the starch granules become swelling. Water molecules can penetrate the inside of starch granules and form hydrogen bonds with the starch molecules. As temperature rises, the original structures of starch granules begin to be destroyed and form more hydrogen bonds with water molecules. When the temperature reaches the pasting temperature, the chain segments inside the starch granules begin to completely unravel. SDS solution is added to modify the starch, and gelatin solutions are used to increase the adhesion between starch granules, which results in a prolonged slow release of colloidosomes. The surface modification leads to the nucleation and growth of particles, ultimately forming into well dispersed starch particles.

Fig. 4a shows the average size of starch particles with different SDS concentrations. As can be seen, the average size of starch particles is 667.5 ± 118.0 nm without adding SDS. When increasing the SDS concentration to 0.2 g/L, the size of starch particles significantly decreases to 200.8 ± 0.2 nm. The size of starch particles slowly reduces as the SDS concentration increases. Fig. 4b displays the Zeta potential of starch particles under varying SDS conditions. In the absence of SDS, the Zeta potential is 4.42 ± 1.94 mV, indicating that the starch solution is positively charged. At a concentration of 0.2 g/L SDS, the Zeta potential changes from positive to negative due to the anionic nature of SDS, which binds to the surface of positively charged starch particles by electrostatic adsorption which creates a negatively charged surface. When the SDS concentration is below the critical ratio, all the added SDS is adsorbed onto the surface of starch particles. Conversely, when the SDS concentration exceeds the critical ratio, SDS molecules are partly dispersed in the aqueous phase and the remaining SDS are adsorbed on starch[44,45]. Therefore, as the SDS concentration grows, the Zeta potential rises rapidly and then slowly. The gradual decrease in the size of starch particles is caused by an increase in SDS adsorption on the surface of starch. This leads to starch molecules with the same charge and repulsion, increasing repulsive force. Additionally, the 12-carbon chain of SDS provides steric stability, further preventing agglomeration and deposition of starch particles [46]. At a concentration of 0.6 g/L, the particles reach a relatively small average size of 120.5 ± 0.5 nm with a cost-effective amount of SDS. Therefore, the optimal SDS addition is 0.6 g/L.

Fig. 4c indicates the SEM image and particle size distribution of the starch dispersions at an SDS concentration of 0.6 g/L. The starch particles are uniformly dispersed, and their particle size distribution is narrow, with an average size of 230 ± 6.0 nm. This size is different from that measured by DLS, which is due to hydrodynamic of the porous particle edge[47]. In addition, the average particle size of localized particles is counted on the TEM images, while the DLS counts the average particle size of a large number of particles. Fig. 4d illustrates the FT-IR spectra of starch particles, SDS, gelatin, and commercial starch. The strong absorption bands in the range of 3700–3000 cm−1 are attributed to the stretching vibration of -OH. The peaks at 2926 cm−1 and 2852 cm−1 are symmetric and antisymmetric peaks for methyl and methylene groups. The characteristic appearance at 1638 cm−1 is due to the stretching and bending vibrational peaks of the hydrogen-bonded O-H group of water[48]. The bending vibrational peaks at 1460 cm−1 are those of the methylene group, and at 1385 cm−1 are those of the methyl group. Therefore, SDS is found to be effectively adsorbed onto the starch surface. There are no additional characteristic peaks observed between the starch particles and gelatin, indicating the absence of any chemical reaction between them.

A schematic diagram of a spray dryer is shown in Fig. 5a. The as-prepared starch dispersions are fed through a peristaltic pump to a nozzle in spray dryer and dispersed into microdroplets by compressed gas. These microdroplets are dried in the drying chamber to form colloidosomes. The drying process is divided into partial and complete drying stages shown in Fig. 5b. The partial drying begins when the microdroplets enter the drying chamber. The hydrophobic part of starch particles and the minimization of total interfacial energy lead the starch colloidal particles to spontaneously move toward the surface of the microdroplets. Simultaneously, the solvents in the microdroplets are partially removed by the hot gas flow, which causes the microdroplets to shrink and the starch particles to contact each other on their surface. The drying process commences with the formation of a shell by the outermost particles. As the solvent evaporates, the starch particles in the solvent continue to move toward the interface, thickening the shell layer. The encapsulated materials in the solvent gradually precipitate until the solvent is completely evaporated. Additionally, due to relatively high temperature of the nozzle, the starch particles in the shell layer can be melted, leading to fusing between the colloidosomes. Fig. 5c indicates the effect of internal and external pressures on the morphology of colloidosomes during the drying process. The pressure inside and outside the colloidosome plays a crucial role in determining the morphology of colloidosomes. When the pressure inside and outside is equal, the sphericity of colloidosomes is improved.

To investigate the preparation conditions of colloidosomes, three parameters were systematically studied: inlet temperature at the nozzle, feed rate, and gas flow rate. Fig. 6a-c contain the SEM images of the colloidosomes under different conditions. As can be seen from Fig. 6a1-a3, when the inlet temperatures are increased from 140 °C to 180 °C, the mean diameter of the colloidosomes decreases from 3.92 ± 1.79 μm to 2.58 ± 0.60 μm with a narrower size distribution, and then increases to 3.53 ± 1.78 μm with a wider size distribution. The morphology of the colloidosomes changes from an incomplete spherical structure to a complete spherical shape, and finally to a donut shape. This may be caused by the complete solvent evaporation in the microdroplets at a low temperature (140 °C). Conversely, at a high temperature (180 °C), the solvent evaporates fast, preventing the particles from moving sufficiently to the gas-liquid interface. This leads to a local imbalance in the microcapsule structure. Regarding the feed rate, as depicted in Fig. 6b1-b3, the colloidosomes exhibit better sphericity, narrower size distribution and smaller size at 4 mL/min, with a mean diameter of 1.74 ± 0.57 μm. As the feeding rate increases, the sphericity of colloidosomes decreases and their size becomes uneven. Furthermore, it can be observed that the morphological changes of colloidosomes are closely linked to the gas flow rate. Fig. 6c1-c3 show the SEM images at different gas flow rates. When increasing the gas flow rate from 5 m3/min to 12 m3/min, the structural integrity is improved and the collapse of spherical colloidosomes is significantly reduced, which leads to a gradual decrease in the mean diameter of the colloidosomes from 2.09 ± 0.49 μm to 1.64 ± 0.32 μm. As the compressed gas disperses the liquids, the smaller and more spherical colloidosomes will be obtained. At a gas flow rate of 12 m3/min, the majority of colloidosomes form relatively small spherical structures (Fig. 6c3), however, the production efficiency is low. Therefore, the optimal process conditions were determined to be an inlet temperature of 160 °C, a feed rate of 4 mL/min, and an air flow rate of 10 m3/min. The obtained colloidosomes have a pore size distribution of 25–40 nm, as shown in Fig. S1.

Fig. 7a and b display the SEM image and size distribution of the PYR encapsulated colloidosomes. After encapsulation, the morphology of colloidosomes has good sphericity without significant changes, and the average particle size is 2.35 ± 0.58 μm. Fig. 7c shows the FT-IR spectra of PYR, blank colloidosomes and PYR loaded colloidosomes. The peaks at 1720 cm−1 and at 1600 cm−1 on the spectrum of PYR loaded colloidosomes are the characteristic absorption peaks for the CO and C-H stretching vibration of carbonyl of the PYR, which demonstrates that the PYR has been successfully encapsulated.

The encapsulation efficiency of PYR loaded colloidosomes is 82 % as measured by UV spectrophotometer and calculated using Eq. 1. Fig. 7d shows the controlled release performance of PYR and PYR colloidosomes with different loading capacities. As a comparison, blank PYR is completely released within 30 min. The controlled release of three kinds of PYR loaded colloidosomes exhibit similar trends but different release percentages. For the PYR loading rate of 4 %, the initial release curve quickly increases in 240 min, reaching 69.7 ± 0.5 %, then gradually slows down until it levels off, at 81.3 ± 0.7 %. This loading rate is selected for further research. Fig. 7e displays the controlled release curves and local amplification of PYR loaded colloidosomes under different pH conditions. The release curves of colloidosomes show similar trends under these three conditions. When the pH is 9, the colloidosome release rate increases fast, reaching 81.8 ± 1.1 % after 9 h and then rises gradually and finally becomes stable, reaching 97.6 ± 1.0 % after 66 h. At a pH of 5, it takes 46 h to release 73.6 ± 1.1 % of PYR. At pH of 7, it takes up to 46 h for the release 86.4 ± 2.3 % of PYR. The maximum release amount of colloidosomes gradually reduces with the decrease of pH, indicating that the colloidosomes have a pH response. Four kinetic models were used to investigate the release behavior (Fig. S2 and Table S1). The segmented Ritger-Pepapes model with the largest correlation coefficient is the best fit (Fig. 7f).

Fig. 8a shows the pH response mechanism of colloidosomes. At a pH of 9, the colloidosome pores are enlarged due to the breaking of hydrogen bonds between starch and gelatin molecules, and the swelling of colloidosomes, reducing the resistance of encapsulated material release. Therefore, the core substance can be released rapidly. However, at a pH of 5, due to the minimal swelling of the colloidosomes and the hydrogen bonding between starch and gelatin molecules, the pore changes of colloidosomes are minimal, which results in the greatest resistance to drug release. Consequently, at a pH of 5, the release of core materials is the slowest. The mechanism can be further verified through permeability experiments using FITC-dextran solution to soak the colloidosomes at different pH and the swelling rate experiments (Fig. 8b and Fig. S3). Fig. S3 displays the swelling rate of PYR loaded colloidosomes under varying pH conditions. With the increase of pH and soaking time, the swelling rate of colloidosomes gradually grows. Fig. 8b presents the CLSM images of PYR loaded colloidosomes at different pH conditions. At pH 5 and 7, the colloidosomes appear black, indicating that the external FITC-dextran are unable to penetrate the colloidosomes pores. At pH 9, the colloidosomes exhibit a bright green color, indicating that the penetration of FITC-dextran pass through the colloidosomes pores. This phenomenon suggests that the pH value can change the size of colloidosomes pores.

Furthermore, the release performances of colloidosomes at varying temperatures were investigated (Fig. S5). The temperature response of colloidosomes is insignificant at a range of 25–35 °C, with only minimal release differences up to 8 h, which may be attributed to the alterations in temperature affecting hydrogen bonding [49] and accelerating intermolecular motion [50].

Some pesticides can be easily decomposed under UV irradiation, and the pesticide activity is weakened. Colloidosomes can be used for encapsulating pesticide molecules in order to prevent UV irradiation and reduce the decomposition of pesticide molecules, which increases the retention rate of pesticide molecules. Therefore, the protection of PYR pesticide molecules by colloidosome was further tested. Fig. 9b displays the graph of the cumulative remaining rate of PYR over time after UV irradiation. The pure PYR decomposes rapidly under UV irradiation. After 8 h, only 1.2 ± 0.2 % of PYR remains undecomposed, indicating that PYR is easy to decompose under UV irradiation. The PYR encapsulated in colloidosomes initially decreases rapidly, with only 77.2 ± 0.7 % of PYR remaining within 2 h and then the retention rate levels off. 72.7 ± 0.9 % of PYR remains undecomposed at 8 h, which shows that the starch colloidosomes can effectively protect PYR molecules.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Expanded applications for loading of Fe3O4 or CDs
ce:section.ce:para: Magnetic response is a valuable property for recovery of colloidosomes. To prepare magnetically responsive colloidosomes, the Fe3O4 nanoparticles were added to the feed solution before the spray drying process. Fig. 10a shows a TEM image of modified Fe3O4 nanoparticles, which are well dispersed with an average particle size of 11.86 ± 2.09 nm. Fig. 10b displays the SEM image of Fe3O4 loaded colloidosomes, showing that the Fe3O4 loaded colloidosomes have good sphericity with particle sizes of 1–5 μm. Fig. 10c illustrates the FT-IR spectra of modified Fe3O4 and Fe3O4 colloidosomes with varying loading rates. The modified Fe3O4 nanoparticles exhibit stretching vibration peaks of Fe-O at 595 cm−1, while the Fe3O4 loaded colloidosomes exhibit a telescopic vibration peak of Fe-O at 582 cm−1. This result confirms the successful encapsulation of Fe3O4 in the colloidosomes.

Fig. 10d shows EDS elemental spectroscopy images of Fe3O4 loaded colloidosomes. It can be observed that Fe elements are homogeneously distributed on the colloidosomes. Fig. 10e illustrates the hysteresis return curves of modified Fe3O4 nanoparticles and colloidosomes with different loading rates and the digital photograph of the adsorption process. The modified Fe3O4 nanoparticles and colloidosomes are superparamagnetic with no noticeable remanent magnetization or coercivity. The saturation magnetization of pure Fe3O4 nanoparticles as a control is 62 emu·g−1. As for the colloidosomes loaded with Fe3O4 nanoparticles, the magnetization increases from 2 emu·g−1 to 5 emu·g−1 as the loading rate increases from 5 % to 15 %. The magnet is capable of collecting nearly all of the dispersed colloidosomes loaded with 5 % Fe3O4 within 3 min in water, which demonstrates the obvious magnetic targeting effect.

Fig. 11a displays the TEM image and particle size distribution of the CDs. Fig. 11b shows the HAADF-STEM image of the CDs. As can be seen, the CDs are spherical in shape and well dispersed, and their average particle size is 3.63 ± 0.75 nm. Fig. 11c shows the SEM image of CD loaded colloidosomes and photographs of CDs and aqueous dispersions of CD loaded colloidosomes under a 365 nm light source. The CD loaded colloidosomes are spherical and have a mean diameter of 2.00 ± 0.81 μm. Compared with the colloidosomes loading with Fe3O4 or PYR, they are similar in size distribution and morphology, indicating that after loading these materials, there is negligible impact on the colloidosomes prepared by this strategy. (Fig. S4) The aqueous dispersions of these colloidosomes also exhibit luminescent properties, indicating that the fluorescence emission centers are derived from the CDs. Fig. 11e presents the FT-IR spectra of the CDs, blank colloidosomes, and CD loaded colloidosomes. The peaks at 1646 cm−1 and at 1562 cm−1 on the spectrum of CD loaded colloidosomes are the characteristic absorption peaks for the CO and CC stretching vibration of CDs. This indicates that the CDs are successfully loaded in the colloidosomes. Fig. 11f shows the XRD patterns of the blank colloidosomes and CD loaded colloidosomes. Both the diffraction peaks appear at 18°, which suggests that the structure of the CDs has not been altered by the encapsulation[43]. The CD loaded colloidosomes can provide a carbon source for plant growth, promoting plant growth.

An efficient and continuous route was proposed for the fabrication of pH-stimuli-responsive starch colloidosomes. The well dispersed starch particles with controllable particle size can be simply prepared through surface modification technology and used as shell materials. Then, the starch colloidosomes were constructed through a spray drying process, which have a regular shape and high sphericity. The optimal conditions, such as an inlet temperature of 160 °C, a feed rate of 4 mL/min and air flow rates of 10 m3/min, can be obtained. After loading PYR, the regular spherical colloidosomes can achieve a small mean diameter of 2.35 ± 0.58 µm with a high encapsulation efficiency of 82 %. The release performance of PYR has an obvious pH response which should be caused by the hydrogen bonds of intermolecular interaction between starch and gelatin, and follows the segmented Ritger-Pepapes model of sustained-release. The as-prepared colloidosomes have a good pH response in the process of PYR controlled release. As the pH is raised from 5 to 9, the maximum release increases from 73.5 ± 1.2 % to 97.6 ± 1.0 %, and the release time increases from 46 to 66 h. The colloidosomes were also used for preventing the photodegradation of pesticide. Under UV irradiation for 8 h, the retention rate of PYR is only 1.2 ± 0.2 %, while that of the PYR loaded colloidosomes is 72.7 ± 0.9 %. This suggests that the starch colloidosomes can protect PYR from photodegradation and have excellent UV shielding performance. In addition, this strategy was used to expand the application scope of the starch colloidosomes. The Fe3O4 and CD loaded colloidosomes are successfully prepared. The Fe3O4 loaded colloidosomes are superparamagnetic with magnetic effects, and the CD loaded colloidosomes can provide the required carbon source for the growth of cash crops.

Qian Sun: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Resources, Project administration, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization. Peng Xie: Writing – review & editing, Methodology. Yuanfeng Pan: Funding acquisition, Supervision, Writing – review & editing. Wei Gao: Writing – review & editing. Zhidan Zheng: Writing – original draft, Methodology. Wenbiao Liu: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Investigation. Xudong Huang: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation. Xia Zhao: Conceptualization. Yimeng Zhang: Writing – original draft, Investigation. Zequan Li: Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (22168010 and 22068006), Guangxi Science and Technology Base and Special Funding for Talents (AD23026333).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at

Adsorption and desorption curves, and pore diameter distribution of colloidosomes; Release kinetics model fitting curves of PYR loaded colloidosomes; Swelling experiment and swelling rate curves of colloidosomes at different pH conditions; Size distribution of colloidosomes loaded with (a) Fe3O4 and (b) CDs; PYR release performance of colloidosomes under different temperature conditions; Table with release kinetics model fitting parameters of PYR loaded colloidosomes.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix B
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.colsurfa.2024.135107.
